preFix.title: String inverters versus microinverters for a home solar PV system
preFix.subtitle: Choosing the inverter architecture that matches your roof and budget
preFix.excerpt: A string inverter converts the DC output of all panels in a series string together. A microinverter sits behind each panel and converts it individually. The choice affects performance under partial shading, installation cost, monitoring granularity, and long-term maintenance. For a simple unshaded south-facing roof, string is cheaper and fine. For any shaded, split-pitch, or multi-orientation roof, microinverters or power optimisers prevent the partial-shade penalty from collapsing the whole array's output.
preFix.sourceNotes: Solar Trade Association (STA) Best Practice Guide for domestic PV 2024. Enphase IQ8 and SolarEdge HD-Wave inverter technical documentation. BEIS Feed-In Tariff and SEG guidance 2024.

content.text: In a string inverter system, all panels are wired in series. Their voltages add together; their current is limited by the weakest panel. If one panel produces 90% of its rated output due to shading. The current of the entire string drops. On a roof with a chimney stack, a dormer. Or overhanging tree branches. This penalty can be substantial. A 20% shading on two panels in a 12-panel string can reduce total output by 40% or more during shadow-passing hours.
All panels face the same direction within 30° of each other.
The roof face is unshaded by adjacent chimneys, dormers, trees. Or neighbouring buildings between 9 am and 3 pm throughout the year.
Budget is the main constraint. String inverters cost much less than microinverters. For a 12-panel array, microinverters add a much premium to the total cost.
Panels split across two roof faces at different orientations, common on L-shaped houses. Each face can be on its own microinverter string and track its own peak.

Any intermittent shading from a chimney, satellite dish, or tree. Even a shadow path crossing one panel for two hours per day on a 12-panel south-facing array can reduce annual yield by 400 to 600 kWh without panel-level

Panel-level monitoring is required, for example, where the owner wants to detect a failing panel quickly. String inverters report total string output. The failure of one panel shows up only as a small overall dip.

A power optimiser gives panel-level MPPT while using a single central inverter for AC conversion. The SolarEdge system is the main UK example. The cost premium is about half that of microinverters. The central inverter is still a single point of failure. With microinverters, a failed unit affects only one panel.
Worked comparison: a Victorian semi in Bristol

South-facing roof, 12 panels, chimney stack shading 2 panels for 3 hours per day on clear days. String inverter annual yield estimate: 3,400 kWh. With microinverters: 3,800 kWh. Difference: 400 kWh per year. The extra yield pays back the microinverter premium in about 11 years. The chimney can be treated as a permanent feature. The microinverter investment pays for itself before the expected 20-year inverter life is reached.
Step-by-step instructions for String inverters versus microinverters for a home solar PV system go here.
